mechanism_narrative: When FEDFUNDS is falling over a short window, markets infer easier policy and lower discount rates; that should lift S&P 500 prices over the next month. The signal is falsified if rate cuts do not coincide with positive 20-day equity returns.
failure_condition: If triggered by monthly FEDFUNDS declines, SPX 20d forward returns are negative in more than 55% of cases or the rolling 3-year rank IC stays below 0.03, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: A rising UNRATE above its recent trend signals worsening labor demand and higher recession risk, which should depress S&P 500 returns over the next month. The mechanism fails if unemployment increases are not followed by negative 20-day equity performance.
failure_condition: Reject if, after a >0.3 percentage point 3-month rise in UNRATE, SPX delivers positive 20d returns in more than 60% of instances or the average conditional return is not negative.

mechanism_narrative: A synchronized improvement in PAYEMS and RSAFS indicates stronger payroll income and retail spending, which should support earnings expectations and produce positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days. The signal is falsified if the joint improvement does not outperform either series alone.
failure_condition: Reject if the joint condition of rising PAYEMS and rising RSAFS does not yield a higher positive-return rate than the unconditional baseline by at least 10 percentage points over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A sustained decline in INDPRO reflects contracting industrial activity and weaker cyclical growth, which should lead S&P 500 returns lower over the next quarter. The claim fails if industrial production weakness is not followed by negative 60-day equity returns.
failure_condition: Reject if quarterly-like 60-day forward returns are not negative on average after 2 consecutive monthly declines in INDPRO, or if the signal's rank IC remains below 0.03.

mechanism_narrative: When HOUST falls sharply, it signals a housing demand contraction that typically spills into construction, credit, and consumption expectations, pushing S&P 500 returns negative over the next month. The mechanism is falsified if housing starts declines do not precede equity weakness.
failure_condition: Reject if, after a >5% two-month decline in HOUST, SPX 20d forward returns are positive in more than 55% of observations or the mean conditional return is nonnegative.

mechanism_narrative: Elevated CPIAUCSL combined with a still-high FEDFUNDS rate signals restrictive real policy that tightens financial conditions and should pressure S&P 500 returns lower over the next 20 days. The effect fails if inflation remains high but policy is not restrictive relative to inflation.
failure_condition: Reject if high CPIAUCSL together with FEDFUNDS above 4.5% does not produce lower-than-baseline 20d SPX returns, or if the conditional hit rate is not at least 5 percentage points below unconditional.

mechanism_narrative: In a recessionary labor regime, a high UNRATE paired with falling PAYEMS captures outright payroll deterioration and jobless stress, which should predict negative S&P 500 returns over the next week. The signal is falsified if labor-market distress does not quickly transmit to equities.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE > 5% and PAYEMS falls 2 straight months
failure_condition: Reject if the triggered condition does not yield negative 5d SPX returns more often than positive returns, or if the conditional average return is not below zero over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A rebound in RSAFS after earlier weakness indicates improving consumer demand and inventory turnover, which should support S&P 500 returns over the next quarter. The hypothesis fails if retail sales recovery does not coincide with sustained positive equity returns.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS rises 2 consecutive months after a 3-month decline
failure_condition: Reject if, after the specified retail sales rebound, SPX 60d forward returns are not positive on average or the hit rate does not exceed 55%.
